questions.question: What is the main weakness of placing keys with `hash(key) % N` when the node count changes?
options: A small topology change can remap a large fraction of keys at once.
It makes keys impossible to hash deterministically.
It prevents any node from receiving traffic.
It automatically keeps replicas in separate failure domains.
questions.explanation: Modulo hashing is simple, but topology changes can cause very disruptive remapping.

questions.question: Why do virtual nodes exist in consistent hashing systems?
options: To smooth ownership balance and reduce the impact of unlucky token spacing.
To guarantee zero data movement when nodes fail.
To remove the need for hashing altogether.
To make replicas unnecessary in distributed storage.
questions.explanation: Virtual nodes help even out distribution across the ring.

questions.question: What is the strongest benefit of consistent hashing in an autoscaled cache cluster?
options: A node add or removal moves only a bounded slice of keys instead of most of them.
Every node instantly gets exactly equal load without virtual nodes.
Clients no longer need any topology information.
The system becomes strongly consistent by default.
questions.explanation: The big win is lower remap churn during topology changes.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of increasing virtual node count aggressively?
options: You often get better balance, but ring state and update overhead increase.
You lose deterministic placement for all keys.
You guarantee every request stays inside one rack.
You eliminate the need for cache warming after remaps.
questions.explanation: More virtual nodes usually smooth balance, but they add control-plane and client overhead.

questions.question: Which observability signal best checks whether a placement change was too disruptive?
options: Remapped-key ratio and miss or migration spikes immediately after the topology update.
The number of code review comments on the rollout document.
The total number of environment variables in the service.
The length of every node hostname.
questions.explanation: Placement churn should be measured directly through remap behavior and its serving impact.

questions.question: Why is consistent hashing alone not enough for durable replica placement?
options: You still need explicit rules to keep replicas out of the same rack, zone, or region.
It cannot assign any primary owner at all.
It prevents background repair from working.
It makes load balancing impossible when capacity differs.
questions.explanation: Replica safety depends on failure-domain awareness, not just deterministic hashing.

questions.question: When would range-based placement sometimes beat a consistent hashing ring?
options: When sequential scans, controlled splits, or explicit ownership ranges matter more than churn-minimizing hashing.
When you never want to rebalance data again.
When all keys must be assigned to every node simultaneously.
When you want every add event to remap the maximum number of keys.
questions.explanation: Range ownership can be better for ordered data, range scans, and explicit split operations.

questions.question: What makes a weighted ring design senior-level rather than superficial?
options: It acknowledges heterogeneous capacity and explains how weights affect balance and failure behavior.
It assumes all machines are identical forever so planning is simpler.
It ignores control-plane rollout because hashing is deterministic.
It treats remap cost as a purely theoretical concern.
questions.explanation: Weighted placement is about real fleet differences and their operational consequences.
